text: This is a gentleman with a left anal hernia.
The surgeon begins dissection by pulling down on the median umbilical ligament, creating a pneumodissection effect that visibly separates the tissue layers.
The surgeon continues to pull down on the median umbilical ligament, attempting to make an initial horizontal incision into the peritoneum.
The surgeon attempts to cut above the area where downward traction is applied, aiming to avoid trailing towards the lateral aspect during the dissection.
The surgeon begins dissecting medially after cutting into the peritoneum, progressing down towards the symphysis pubis.
The surgeon uses two instruments with their tips together, positioning the fenestrated bipolar slightly upwards to avoid injury to the bladder during dissection.
The surgeon approaches the symphysis pubis medially and then works laterally, continuing the dissection technique previously used.
The surgeon uses the tip of the fenestrated bipolar instrument to grasp the edge of the peritoneum against the pelvic wall, followed by blunt dissection to separate the tissue layers.

The surgeon uses the fenestrated bipolar instrument to reach deep enough to touch the pelvic wall, allowing them to grasp the edge of the peritoneum and pull it inferiorly and back towards themselves.
The surgeon manipulates the peritoneum, pulling it inferiorly and slightly superiorly, while pointing out the location of the femoral nerve.
The surgeon carefully maintains the dissection along the peritoneum, ensuring the instruments remain close to avoid injury to the nearby nerves.
The surgeon uses the fenestrated bipolar to meet the dissected peritoneum edges in the middle, completing the dissection from medial and lateral approaches.

The surgeon identifies the inferior epigastric vessels slightly to the left of the dissection area, revealing a direct defect. The dissection progresses inferiorly to create the deepest margins, exposing the cord structures and vasculature to the testicle.
The surgeon begins to expose the symphysis pubis, carefully dissecting one to two centimeters inferior to this area to ensure proper placement of the mesh.
The surgeon dissects across the midline to the opposite side, revealing the pubic tubercle at the midline, which serves as a landmark indicating sufficient dissection to the opposite side.
The surgeon dissects fat from the femoral canal, revealing the iliac/femoral vein at the lateral border of the canal.
The femoral vein becomes visible as the surgeon continues to clear surrounding tissue, enhancing the exposure of the vein.
The surgeon dissects inferior to the symphysis pubis, revealing the obturator foramen in the surgical field.
The surgeon inspects the lateral border of the indirect defect for a lipoma of the cord, observing minimal presence of tissue in this area.
The surgeon continues the inferior dissection, retracting the peritoneum to ensure the mesh can be placed flat in the dissected area.
The surgeon peels the peritoneum off the area of a small indirect defect, revealing underlying structures.

The surgeon identifies the cord structures moving medially to the right, with vessels positioned laterally and the edge of the peritoneum visible inferiorly. Dissection continues to create sufficient space for placing a twelve by sixteen piece of mesh.

The surgeon uses the tips of scissors to grasp and dissect fatty tissue, pulling it medially away from the vessels. The edge of the peritoneum is visible, and the dissection continues to create sufficient space for the mesh placement.
The surgeon gently pulls fatty tissue away from the iliac vein, focusing on the medial and inferior area to ensure complete clearance for proper mesh placement.

The surgeon identifies an indirect defect, a direct defect higher up medially, and a femoral defect. The iliac vein and obturator foramen are visible, along with the symphysis pubis across the midline. The dissection is complete, preparing the area for mesh placement.
The surgeon prepares a twelve by sixteen centimeter pro-grit mesh, marking its center to align with the center of the defects for optimal coverage.

The surgeon positions the marked center of the mesh near the inferior epigastric vessels, ensuring it is fully opened from medial to lateral. The mesh is laid with ample space, with the peritoneum dissected approximately one centimeter away from the mesh, providing a clear area for placement.
The mesh is positioned inferior to the symphysis pubis, ensuring that when the bladder expands, it will trap the mesh against the symphysis pubis.
The surgeon positions the mesh on the superior side, which is visually easier to manage due to the absence of critical structures in that area.

The surgeon marks a line across the mesh to indicate the remaining fold needed for placement. Slight bleeding is visible, which can obscure the markings, but the surgeon uses this method to ensure accurate mesh positioning.

The surgeon confirms that the crosshatch on the mesh is positioned at the center of the myopic tinea orifice. Using a GS-XXII needle with a two-oh V-lock suture, the surgeon demonstrates a suturing technique by threading the suture through both edges of the peritoneum, manipulating the needle with alternating thumb positions, and sweeping with a fenestrated bipolar instrument.
This saves the number of touches on the needle and makes you quite efficient in your suturing.

The surgeon ensures the peritoneum is completely closed with sutures to prevent small bowel obstruction. Extra sutures are placed for practice. The needle is carefully withdrawn, with the trocar opening blocked to prevent accidental ejection.
